coredata.dc:description: Electrocatalytic dual electron oxygen reduction to hydrogen peroxide (2e ORR) is considered a special and sustainable method for on-site continuous production of hydrogen peroxide. Oxygen doped carbon based materials have demonstrated their superiority in the generation of H2O2. However, there is still a lack of direct experimental evidence to determine the true active site on complex carbon surfaces. In this work, we introduce only carboxyl functional groups on the surface of carbon dots by a mild and simple synthesis method to study the specific COOH functional groups on the generation of H2O2. The H2O2 selectivity was correlated with the contents of COOH functional groups. In alkaline environments, the citric acid modified CDs (CA-OCDs) own excellent catalytic ability for 2e ORR, showing a maximum starting potential of 0.829 V (vs. RHE) and a high selectivity of 89.7 % over a wide potential range of 0.2–0.7 V (vs. RHE). In actual H2O2 production, the Faraday efficiency (FE) of CA-OCDs can be up to 97 %, and the yield achieves 838 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1. This work provides an effective and simple strategy for designing efficient carbon-based catalysts for sustainable H2O2 electrosynthesis.

head.ce:title: Surface state modulation of carbon dots for efficient generation of hydrogen peroxide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin, 300130, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin, 300130, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrocatalytic dual electron oxygen reduction to hydrogen peroxide (2e ORR) is considered a special and sustainable method for on-site continuous production of hydrogen peroxide. Oxygen doped carbon based materials have demonstrated their superiority in the generation of H2O2. However, there is still a lack of direct experimental evidence to determine the true active site on complex carbon surfaces. In this work, we introduce only carboxyl functional groups on the surface of carbon dots by a mild and simple synthesis method to study the specific COOH functional groups on the generation of H2O2. The H2O2 selectivity was correlated with the contents of COOH functional groups. In alkaline environments, the citric acid modified CDs (CA-OCDs) own excellent catalytic ability for 2e ORR, showing a maximum starting potential of 0.829 V (vs. RHE) and a high selectivity of 89.7 % over a wide potential range of 0.2–0.7 V (vs. RHE). In actual H2O2 production, the Faraday efficiency (FE) of CA-OCDs can be up to 97 %, and the yield achieves 838 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1. This work provides an effective and simple strategy for designing efficient carbon-based catalysts for sustainable H2O2 electrosynthesis.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), as an important preservative and oxidant, has been widely used in chemical synthesis, wastewater treatment, paper and textile manufacturing, and even as a promising energy carrier [1–5]. The huge demand for H2O2 makes it as one of the 100 most important chemicals in the world [6]. Current industrial processes for synthesizing H2O2 involve energy-intensive anthraquinone oxidation/reduction, which requires complex and large-scale infrastructure and generates large amounts of waste chemicals [7]. In the presence of a catalyst [8–10], direct synthesis of H2O2 from hydrogen and oxygen provides a more direct pathway, ideally solving the problems associated with the indirect anthraquinone method. However, this process involves the risk of explosion of H2 and O2 mixtures, which is difficult to control [9,11,12]. Therefore, driven by the urgent demand for more environmentally friendly processes, the electrochemical synthesis of H2O2 through the two-electron oxygen reduction reaction (2e ORR) has shown great prospects due to its safe, green, efficient, and economical characteristics [13,14]. In addition, it can be realized through electrochemical energy systems, such as fuel cells and metal air batteries, which not only ensures the on-site production of H2O2 by portable equipment, but also provides simultaneous power generation [15,16]. However, 2e ORR is hindered by the competitive four-electron oxygen reduction reaction (4e ORR) [17–19].

Generally, cathode catalytic materials are mainly used to regulate the adsorption energy of O2 and intermediates, and high activity and selectivity of catalysts is a prerequisite for electrochemical production of H2O2 [12,20]. Therefore, it is highly necessary to design and develop efficient oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) electrocatalysts for selective production of H2O2 through the 2e pathway. Precious metal based catalysts, such as Pd based [17,21,22] and Au based [23] materials, exhibit high H2O2 selectivity over a wide pH range, but their low abundance and high cost limit further large-scale applications. In contrast, carbon based materials not only have abundant reserves, but also have adjustable nanostructures/interfaces, good chemical stability, which shows great potential in H2O2 electrosynthesis [24–26]. However, it is still a big challenge to design carbon based catalysts with high efficient generation of H2O2, because they only exhibit reasonable Faraday efficiency within a narrow application potential range under alkaline conditions, but also have low productivity in bulk electrolysis [27].

Recent studies have shown that oxygen functionalization can modulate the ORR pathway by altering the local electronic structure of the carbon materials surface, thus affecting the binding energy between carbon and intermediates [16,28]. In general, strong acid chemistry is usually employed in order to introduce oxygen functional groups into a perfect carbon structure, which always leads to a mixture of different types of functional groups, so that the active site induced by oxygen functionalization remains elusive. As in previous studies, various oxygen functional groups, including ether (C–O–C) [26,29,30], epoxy group [26], carbonyl/quinone (C=O) [24,31], and carboxyl groups (O–C=O) [31,32] have been suggested to be the active site. However, it is still unclear of the relationship for these groups and which is more efficient in the production process of H2O2. Beside element doping, surface modification is also an effective method to introduce oxygen functional groups [33]. Among all carbon nanomaterials with various topological nanostructures, carbon dots (CDs) are a unique zero dimensional nanomaterial with abundant edge sites [34,35]. These edge sites are highly advantageous for functional group modification, but there are currently few reports to investigate the specific functional groups of CDs on the production of H2O2 through the 2e pathway, and it is significant to disclose the surface state modulation mechanism of CDs for generation of H2O2 via 2e ORR.

Compared to other functional groups such as carbonyl groups (such as formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, etc.) and hydroxyl groups (methanol), carboxyl functional groups are relatively safe, green and common, many organic compounds and biomolecules contain carboxyl functional groups, which are easier to obtain in nature and chemical synthesis. More importantly, as electron withdrawing groups, carboxyl groups can effectively adjust the electronic structure of carbon dots [36].

Here, we used chemical method to prepare raw CDs and only introduce carboxyl functional groups on the surface of CDs to regulate the 2e ORR for boosting the efficiency of H2O2. Different molecules with COOH groups were used to modulate the surface states of CDs without damaging the carbon core, which greatly improves the hydrogen peroxide yield and selectivity of the CDs. In addition, we found correlation between the H2O2 selectivity of the CDs and the contents of COOH groups. We further proved that the carbon atom connected to the –COOH groups is the active site for oxygen reduction reaction by mild reduction of sodium borohydride. The CA-OCDs exhibit approximately 89.7 % selectivity for H2O2 production over a wide potential range. In addition, the CA-OCDs have good application in actual H2O2 production, with Faraday efficiency (FE) of 97 %, a yield of 838 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1, and good stability at least 10 h. This work provides a facile method to introduce specific active sites and rational design carbon based catalysts for production of H2O2 with high selectivity and Faraday efficiency.

Sodium dihydrogen phosphate was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Science and Technology Development Co. Oxalic acid (EA) was obtained from Fuchen (Tianjin) Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. Citric acid (CA) was bought from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technology Co., LTD. Ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid was purchased from McLean's Inc. Hydroquinone (HQ) was obtained from Tianjin Aoqilopu Trading Co. Terephthalic acid (TPA) was purchased from Shanghai Dibai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Terephthalaldehyde (Tere) was obtained from Bide Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. The graphite rod was purchased from Tianjin Zhongnuo Carbon Factory. Cerium sulfate was bought from Kaimat Tianjin Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Deionized water was used in the experiment. All chemicals are used without purification.

Original CDs (OCDs) without surface modification were prepared by electrolysis method [37]. With the graphite rod as the carbon source, sodium dihydrogen phosphate (400 mg) was dissolved in the deionized water (200 mL) as the electrolyte. The static potential (20 V) was provided with DC power supply between the two graphite rods. Prior to the reaction, carbon rods (0.5 cm in diameter and 10 cm in length) were sonicated in anhydrous ethanol and deionized water sequentially for half an hour to remove surface impurities. After that, two carbon rods were vertically inserted into the electrolyte with a distance of 1 cm. The DC voltage of 20 V was added on the carbon rods. During the reaction process, the solution was continuously stirred with magneton, and the color of the solution was changed from colorless to yellow and ultimately to brownish black. Then, the brown black solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min to remove large carbon particles. Finally, the solution was freeze dried to obtain OCDs.

In order to investigate the relationship between the surface carboxyl groups and their catalytic performance, We chose small molecules of different relative molecular weights containing different numbers of carboxyl functional groups to modify OCDs. First, 20 mg OCDs and 50 mg citric acid (CA) were added to 40 mL deionized water and stirred thoroughly for 24 h to obtain CA modified OCDs (CA-OCDs). After washing several times with water/ethanol by centrifuging at 12,000 rpm, CA-OCDs (CA = 50 mg) were obtained by freeze-drying. The EA-OCDs (EA = 50 mg) was synthesized by replacing CA with oxalic acid (EA), the EDTA-OCDs (EDTA = 50 mg) was prepared by changing CA with ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), while the HQ-OCDs (HQ = 50 mg), TPA-OCDs (TPA = 50 mg) and Tere-OCDs (Tere = 50 mg) were synthesized via replacing CA with hydroquinone (HQ), terephthalic acid (TPA), and para benzaldehyde (Tere), respectively, and the rest of the reaction conditions were the same as CA-OCDs. Meanwhile, CA-OCDs-x, EA-OCDs-x and EDTA-OCDs-x were prepared by the same method (x = 10, 30, 70, 100 mg), where x represented the content of CA, EA and EDTA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical reduction of surface modified OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the role of carboxyl functional groups, prepared CDs were reduced with NaBH4 to remove partial carboxyl groups on CDs. First, 20 mg CA-OCDs were added to 20 mL deionized aqueous solution and stirred at 25 °C for 30 min, then it was heated to 80 °C and added 20 mg NaBH4 under agitation for 3 h [38]. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm and washed with water/ethanol several times. Finally, R-CA-OCDs were obtained by freeze-drying. OCDs, EA-OCDs and EDTA-OCDs were reduced with the same process and R-OCDs, R-EA-OCDs and R-EDTA-OCDs were obtained, respectively.

The phase structure of the prepared CDs was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8-Discover). The morphology and lattice spacing of the prepared samples were determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200S). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured with a spectrophotometer (Hitachi, U–3900H). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Vetext-BRUKER) and Raman spectroscopy (inVia-Reflex) were used to analyze the chemical bonds of the samples and the degree of defects for the CDs, respectively. The chemical bonding states of each element in the samples were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 Xi).

Electrochemical measurements were performed using a rotating disc electrode instrument (RRDE-3A) equipped with an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760 E). Using a three electrode system, the RRDE electrode of the Glassy carbon electrode (Disc diameter 3.0 mm) coated with the prepared CDs and the platinum ring electrode (inner diameter 5.0 mm, outer diameter 7.0 mm, Disc diameter 4.0 mm) are used as the working electrode, the graphite rod was used as the counter electrode to form a current loop, and the Saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as the reference electrode, respectively. 5 mg of catalyst, 500 μL isopropanol, 465 μL deionized water and 35 μL 5 wt% Nafion membrane solution were mixed and sonicated for 30 min to prepare catalyst ink. Firstly, polish the surface of the glassy carbon electrode to a bright state, and then the catalyst ink (11 μL) was dripped onto the glassy carbon disk electrode and dried at room temperature. All electrochemical measurements were conducted in a 0.1 M N2/O2 saturated KOH solution (pH = 13). Electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was measured by cyclic voltammetry with different scan rates (2 mV s−1, 4 mV s−1, 6 mV s−1, 8 mV s−1 and 10 mV s−1) in the non-Faraday range (1.0–1.1 V vs RHE). H2O2 selectivity was obtained from the polarization curve under O2 saturation conditions with a scanning rate of 5 mV s−1 at 1600 rpm. According to previous reports [39–41], the annular electrode was set at a constant potential of 0.50 V vs. SCE (or +1.48 V vs. RHE) to detect the generated H2O2. The H2O2 selectivity and electron transfer number (n) on RRDE can be calculated using the following equation:

where IR represents the ring current, ID is the absolute value of the disk current, and the collection efficiency (N) of the RRDE is determined to be 0.37.

The bulk measurements were taken with a typical H-type battery. The cell contains 75 mL of electrolyte separated by a membrane on an electrochemical workstation (Nafion 117), which was pretreated at 80 °C for 1 h in 5 wt% H2O2 aqueous solution, followed by further 10 min at 80 °C in ultrapure water. Carbon cloth (1.0 × 1.0 cm2) was used as working electrode, catalyst load was 0.05 mg cm−2, saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and graphite rod were used as reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. Prior to measurement, the electrolyte in the cathode chamber is purged with O2 for at least 30 min to achieve saturation, and vigorously stirred to promote O2 mass transfer.

The concentration of H2O2 was quantitatively determined by Ce4+ titration (

) [42]. When H2O2 was added, the Ce4+ ion solution was yellow while the Ce3+ ion solution was colorless. The absorbance curve versus H2O2 concentration was drawn by linearly fitting the absorbance values at 319 nm for known concentrations of Ce4+ ions with 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5 mM. By thoroughly mixing the obtained solution with 0.5 mM Ce4+ ion solution and measuring the absorbance at 319 nm to determine the Ce4+ concentration, the H2O2 yield is determined based on the reduced Ce4+ concentration. The Faraday efficiency (FE) generated by H2O2 in H cells can be calculated as follows:
(3)
FE
( % )
= 2 CFV / Q × 100 %
where F is the Faraday's constant (96,485 C mol−1), C is the concentration of H2O2, V is the volume of the electrolyte, and Q is the total charge during the ORR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology of prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we synthesized CDs by electrochemical method. Due to the abundant edge sites of CDs, they can attract carboxyl containing small molecules (EA, CA, and EDTA) to the surface of CDs through longer stirring time, resulting in van der Waals interactions and realizing the modification of the surface functional groups. Figure S1 and Fig. 1 show the TEM and HRTEM images of OCDs, EA-OCDs, CA-OCDs, and EDTA-OCDs, and it can be seen that the four CDs are monodisperse nanoparticles. According to the particle size distribution (inset of Fig. 1 ), the average particle size of four CDs is between 1.0 nm and 3.2 nm. The average particle sizes of OCDs, EA-OCDs, CA-OCDs, and EDTA-OCDs are 2.10 ± 0.17 nm, 2.23 ± 0.26 nm, 2.13 ± 0.21 nm and 1.98 ± 0.28 nm, respectively. EA-OCDs, CA-OCDs and EDTA-OCDs show similar structures and size distribution, indicating that the effect of different molecules on morphology of CDs is negligible, further excluding the morphological effect on the change of 2e ORR properties. Additionally, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images show that the lattice stripe spacing of OCDs

is about 0.21 nm and 0.34 nm, respectively, corresponding to the (100) and (002) crystal planes in the graphite structure (Fig. S2) [43]. The optical properties of prepared OCDs were determined by UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra. As shown in Fig. S3b, the photoluminescent excitation and emission peaks of OCDs are located at 340 and 440 nm, respectively. The UV–vis spectrum of OCDs has an absorption peak between 250 and 300 nm, which is attributed to the π–π* transition of C=C bonds [44]. Meanwhile, in the XRD patterns of the OCDs, the presence of broad peaks at 2θ = 23° and 42° corresponds to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of the graphitic carbon, respectively (Fig. S3d) [45,46].

It has been reported that defects in carbon materials can be served as reaction sites for oxygen adsorption or reduction during electrocatalysis [47]. Fig. 2 a shows the Raman spectra of OCDs, EA-OCDs, CA-OCDs and EDTA-OCDs, the Raman peaks centered at 1339 and 1586 cm−1 are corresponded to the D (defect) and G (graphite) bands of the carbon materials, respectively. The intensity ratio of D to G bands (ID/IG) is commonly used to assess the graphitization degree of carbon materials. The ID/IG values of OCDs are approximately 1.08, while the ID/IG values of EA-OCDs, CA-OCDs, and EDTA-OCDs are approximately 1.06, 1.07, and 1.10. The ID/IG values of these three CDs are almost the same as OCDs, indicating that they have similar disordered structures. The surface modification does not change the graphitization degree, which rules out the effect of defects on the ORR process. The surface
modulation with adjustable COOH contents can be realized without destroying the carbon skeleton.

In order to study their chemical and physical properties, the surface groups of four CDs were investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). As shown in Fig. 2b, all spectra exhibit a broad band around 3400 cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching vibration of O–H bonds, confirming the presence of –OH groups [48]. The peaks located at 1574 and 1220 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C=C and C–O bonds, respectively [49]. The peak at 1715 cm−1 is derived from the C=O vibration in carboxylic acid (COOH), which confirms the existence of COOH groups [50,51]. The relative intensities of the carboxyl groups at 1715 cm−1 are changed with the addition of different molecules, compared with the relative intensities of the carboxyl groups for OCDs, EA-OCDs, CA-OCDs, and EDTA-OCDs were significantly enhanced, which suggests that the molecules are successfully attached to the surface of OCDs.

In addition, the XPS spectra in Fig. S4 further verify the coexistence of O and C elements in the four CDs, and two characteristic peaks at 533 eV and 283 eV are corresponded to O 1s and C 1s, respectively [18,52]. In particular, the atomic oxygen/carbon ratio changes with the addition of different molecules, indicating that the substituents are successfully attached to the OCDs surface. And the oxygen contents of OCDs, EA-OCDs, CA-OCDs, and EDTA-OCDs are 18.50 %, 18.53 %, 20.28 %, and 26.38 %, respectively (Table S1), the oxygen content gradually increased with the modification of EA, CA, and EDTA. Fig. 2c and d shows the high-resolution C 1s and O1s spectra and fitting curves of four types of CDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2c, the C1s spectra can be decomposed into five curves centered around 284.6, 285.2, 286.0, 287.0, and 288.7 eV, corresponding to C–C, C–N, -C-OH, –C=O, and –COOH bonds, respectively [53], respectively. The bonding states of oxygen species in all samples, are shown in Fig. 2d, the O 1s signal of four CDs can be fitted with three curves centered at 531.7, 532.8, and 533.5 eV, corresponding to the –C=O, –COOH, and -C-OH groups [54–57], respectively. According to the oxygen contents and fitting results, the contents of different oxygen-containing functional groups are shown in Table 1 . It is worth noting that the relative contents of COOH groups are gradually increased with the addition of different molecules, and the relative contents of carboxyl groups in OCDs, EA-OCDs, CA-OCDs, and EDTA-OCDs are 32.77 %, 33.81 %, 38.19 %, and 42.84 %, respectively.

This may be due to the van der Waals forces between EA, CA, EDTA molecules and CDs. Generally, for substances with similar composition and structure, the van der Waals forces between molecules increase with the increased relative molecular weight [58]. The van der Waals forces between EA, CA, EDTA molecules and OCDs are different, which leads to the different COOH contents bound to OCDs by different surface modifiers. Besides, the surface carboxyl content of OCDs, EA-OCDs, CA-OCDs, and EDTA-OCDs was analyzed by using the TPD-MS method, in which the MS signal versus temperature curves for CO2 are shown in Fig. S5. It can be seen that the broad CO2 peaks in the range of 100–400 °C for the OCDs, EA-OCDs, CA-OCDs and EDTA-OCDs samples are attributed to the decomposition of the carboxyl groups on the surface [59], with more decomposition occurring around 150 °C. With the addition of small molecules such as EA, CA, and EDTA, the carboxyl group content on the surface of carbon dots gradually increases, which is consistent with the XPS results.

It is well known that oxygen-containing functional groups play an important role in the electrosynthesis of H2O2 for carbon based catalysts [24,26,30]. We selected small molecules with carboxyl functional groups and modified the CDs by van der Waals interactions. In order to investigate the relationship between COOH groups and H2O2 productivity, we specifically studied the catalytic activity and selectivity of different contents of EA, CA and EDTA for oxygen reduction using a rotating ring disk electrode, and determined a collection efficiency of 0.37 (Fig. S6). All the tests were conducted in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte saturated with O2. The LSV curves of the four CDs are shown in Figs. S7–S9. The upper part (dotted line) of the LSV curve represents the ring current density, while the lower part (solid line) shows the disk current density. It can be seen that the selectivity of H2O2 increases first and then decreases with the increased contents of small molecules. This indicates that carboxylic acid functionalization can successfully regulate the selectivity of O2 towards H2O2. Subsequently, we compared the optimized OCDs, EA-OCDs (EA-OCDs-50), CA-OCDs (CA-OCDs-50), and EDTA-OCDs (EDTA-OCDs-50) samples (Fig. 3 a). At a current density of 0.1 mA cm−2, CA-OCDs showed the highest initial potential (relative to RHE) of 0.829 V (Fig. S10), which is higher than the thermodynamic equilibrium 2e ORR potential (0.75 V) and is consistent with previous studies [17,60–62]. This may be due to the carboxylic functional group –COOH losing its proton (H+) in solution to form carboxylate ions (-COO-) [63]. When carboxyl groups modify CDs, a large number of carboxylate ions are formed on the surface of the CDs, resulting in a high local alkaline environment on the surface of the CDs. The ion activity changes, which in turn causes the phenomenon of Nernst related potential shift [64].

In addition, the CA-OCDs achieve a maximum ring current (dashed, H2O2 oxidation current) in the potential range of 0.2–0.8 V (vs RHE), which indicates that more H2O2 is produced on the CA-OCDs. Notably, the order of increase in Iring is from OCD to EA-OCD, EDTA-OCDs to CA-OCD, suggesting that H2O2 synthesis is progressively promoted with increasing oxygen content (Fig. S11a). Then, the selectivity of H2O2 and the electron transfer number of ORR are calculated based on the LSV curves. As shown in Fig. 3b and c, CA-OCDs exhibit the highest H2O2 selectivity at 0.2–0.7 V (vs RHE), and achieve to nearly 90 % H2O2 selectivity (89.7 %) between 0.55 and 0.65 V (vs RHE). This indicates that the ORR catalyzed by CA-OCDs is almost dominated by the 2e pathway. The selectivity of H2O2 can be enhanced from 44.16 % for OCDs to 89.7 % for CA-OCDs, corresponding to the increased contents of carboxyl groups from 32.77 % in OCDs to 42.84 % in CA-OCDs (Fig. S11b), which suggests that the carboxyl group plays an important role in surface-modulated CDs and that the carboxyl group content correlates with the selectivity of hydrogen peroxide. In addition, the electrons transfer number at 0.6 V follow the order of CA-OCDs (n = 2.21) < EA-OCDs (n = 2.69) < OCDs (n = 3.13), further confirming the positive influence of carboxyl groups on the selectivity of the 2e pathway of CDs in the ORR process [65,66]. In addition, the Iring of EDTA-OCDs

is lower than that of CA-OCDs, and the electron transfer number (n = 2.38) is similar to that of CA-OCDs, while the selectivity is slightly lower. This indicates a reduction in activity and selectivity after further increasing the oxygen content to higher level, which may be induced by the decrease of electrical conductivity with excessive oxygen modification [67]. In the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of Fig. S12, CA-OCDs show smaller semicircle compared to EDTA-OCDs, indicating a lower charge transfer resistance. Besides, Fig. 3d shows that the Tafel slope of CA-OCDs (98.73 mV dec−1) caused by H2O2 oxidation current is lower than that of OCDs (121.67 mV dec−1), EA-OCDs (108.60 mV dec−1), and EDTA-OCDs (103.57 mV dec−1). In general, a smaller Tafel slope indicates faster reaction kinetics. Therefore, when the H2O2 oxidation reaction occurs on the same Pt ring electrode, the rapid kinetics reflect the high formation rate of H2O2 catalyzed by the CA-OCDs. This rapid kinetics indicates that CA-OCDs can be a promising candidate for the synthesis of H2O2 in alkaline fuel cell. Subsequently, we investigated the electrocatalytic activity of the CA-OCDs with the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA). The ECSA can be determined using a double layer capacitance (Cdl), and the higher Cdl values disclose greater ECSA and higher catalytic activity of the catalyst (Fig. S13). Fig. 3e shows the Cdl values of four CDs and the CA-OCDs have higher Cdl value of 7.41 mF cm−2 than that of OCDs (3.07 mF cm−2), EA-OCDs (3.55 mF cm−2), and EDTA-OCDs (4 mF cm−2), which suggests that CA-OCDs own the best catalytic ability among these samples. The Cdl value is nearly twice as high as that of OCDs, which may be due to the fact that the carboxyl functional group has a negative charge modifying the surface of the CDs and increases the charge density on the surface of the CDs [68]. In addition, the performance of CA-OCDs is superior to most reported carbon based electrocatalysts (Fig. 3f and Table S2).

Confirming the active functional groups through experiments is a challenging task. Here, in order to further confirm the activity and selectivity of oxygen functional groups (-COOH) in electrochemical oxygen reduction to produce H2O2, we chose NaBH4 to remove some carboxyl groups on prepared CDs [38,69]. As shown in the C 1s and O 1s XPS spectra of R-OCDs, R-EA-OCDs, R-CA-OCDs, and R-EDTA-OCDs (Fig. 4 a and b), the relative content of carboxyl groups in the four samples was significantly lower than that of OCDs (Table S3), further confirming the successful reduction of carboxyl groups by NaBH4. The ORR performance of R-OCDs, R-EA-OCDs, R-CA-OCDs, and R-EDTA-OCDs samples is shown in Fig. 4c. Compared with Fig. 3a, the initial potential and ultimate current density significantly increase after chemical reduction. However, it is worth noting that as shown in Fig. 4d and S13a , R-OCDs, R-EA-OCDs, R-CA-OCDs, and R-EDTA-OCDs show a more significant decrease in hydrogen peroxide yield, indicating that chemical reduction eliminated some active sites. In addition, the electron transfer number of R-OCDs is close to 4 (Fig. S14b), indicating that the mechanism type and selectivity of ORR can be driven by the presence or absence of modified oxygen functional groups on CDs [70,71]. As expected, the hydrogen peroxide selectivity of R-CA-OCDs was significantly lower than that of CA-OCDs. These results indicate that the carboxyl COOH functional groups on CDs play an important role in the catalytic activity of CDs (Fig. 4e).

To further validate these results, we investigated independent molecules with phenolic, carboxylic, and aldehyde groups, such as hydroquinone, terephthalic acid, and paraphthalaldehyde. As shown in Fig. S15, the hydrogen peroxide yield of terephthalic acid is higher than that of other molecules and TPA has the smallest Tafel slope with faster electrochemical reaction kinetics compared to other functional group-modified OCDs, which further proves that –COOH group has a greater promotion effect on catalytic activity. At the same time, we have conducted density functional theory calculations on CDs modified with different oxygen-containing functional groups to explore the sources of activity. Using carbon dots as a model system, different types of oxygen-containing functional groups, including carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups, are modified on CDs (Figs. S16–S19). The ORR activity of a catalyst depends on the ability of the intermediates (OOH*, O*, and OH*) to bind to the active site of the catalyst (carbon atoms indicated by red circles in the figure). For two-electron ORR, the generation of hydrogen peroxide occurs through the following pathways:
(4)
O 2
+
H 2
O 2
+
e −
→ O O
H *
+ O
H −
(5)
O O
H *
+
e −
→ H
O 2 −

Among them, OOH* intermediates play an important role in the generation of di-electronic H2O2, and the adsorption energy of OOH* (ΔGOOH*) was used as a descriptor for evaluating the catalytic activity of different active sites [72]. We calculated the ΔGOOH* of all possible active sites of carbon dots modified with different oxygen-containing functional groups, and compared the free energy at their optimal positions, as shown in Fig. 5 . Among different carbon dots, COOH modified CDs have the smallest free energy barrier for reducing O2 to H2O2, and the ΔGOOH* value (3.97 eV) is closest to the ideal value of 3.52 eV, indicating that COOH modified CDs achieve the optimal balance of the adsorption and desorption ability of the active site for OOH*, which is beneficial for the preservation of O–O bonds and the generation of H2O2 [73,74]. The carboxyl functional group is a stronger electron-withdrawing group with higher electron affinity which accepts electrons more readily [75–77]. This allows less electron transfer to H2O2 and blocks the four electron reaction. Some calculations [30,57,78] have also demonstrated that carboxyl COOH functional groups can reduce the energy barrier of the HOOH * → H2O2 rate determination step by adjusting the electronic structure of carbon materials,

changing the charge of active sites, and optimizing the binding energy between carbon sites and intermediate OOH*, thereby promoting the occurrence of two-electron reactions. The COOH group contributes to the activity of H2O2 production, and the carbon atoms connected to the COOH group are considered as the most active sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bulk H2O2 electrosynthesis and long-term stability
ce:section.ce:para: Large-scale hydrogen peroxide production is essential in many industries, including medicine and health, energy carriers, and food processing [79]. Therefore, we performed batch H2O2 electrosynthesis in an oxygen-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte using an H-cell structure (Fig. S20) as the working electrode and loaded the catalyst ink onto carbon paper with an area of 1 × 1 cm2 and quantified the H2O2 yield by dicerium sulfate titration [80]. The working electrode (WE) coated with CA-OCDs and the counter electrode (CE) are separated through a Nafion membrane. Conduct constant potential chronoamperometry was tested at 0.6 V vs. RHE. Considering that the accumulation of H2O2 during long-term operation can easily interfere with oxygen reduction on the catalyst modified electrode in the cathode chamber, the electrolyte was exchanged with fresh electrolyte every 2 h and the resulting electrolyte was refreshed. Fig. S21 shows the TEM images of CA-OCDs after the 2-h stability test, indicating that the morphology of CA-OCDs did not undergo significant changes. And after replacing the electrolyte, the current density is restored again, disclosing that CA-OCDs can be recycled in alkaline electrolytes and maintain good stability.
Fig. 6 a shows the long-term testing of CA-OCDs for 10 h, and the current

density of all long-term CA curves is almost the same after 10 h, indicating that CA-OCDs have excellent stability. After characterizing the performance of the CA-OCDs for 10 h and calculating the corresponding H2O2 selectivity and electron transfer number, the hydrogen peroxide yield of the CA-OCDs reaches 83 % after 10 h, as shown in Fig. S22. Then, the concentration of H2O2 produced by CA-OCDs was determined by cerium sulfate titration. Accordingly, the standard concentration curve of Ce(SO4)2 solution was fitted (Fig. S23). As shown in Fig. 6b, the maximum H2O2 of CA-OCDs body can achieve 838 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1.

Fig. 6c gives the average H2O2 Faraday efficiency (FE) of CA-OCDs at different times and it can reach a maximum FE of 97 %, which demonstrates the positive role of CA-OCDs in improving the catalytic activity and selectivity of electrochemical H2O2 production. Compared to the previous reports, the CA-OCDs still show advantages in terms of H2O2 yield and FE achieved in H-type battery (Table S4).

In summary, carboxyl functional groups of different molecules were successfully introduced on OCDs using a mild and simple synthesis method, and further functionalization conferred the carbon in the dominant COOH group as active sites for catalyzed H2O2 production by ORR. Among them, CA-OCDs own relatively high activity and selectivity, which shows great potential in electrochemical production of hydrogen peroxide. CA modified OCDs can increase the selectivity from about 45 % for OCDs to about 90 % for CA-OCDs. In the bulk H2O2 production, H2O2 output can achieve 838 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1, and the maximum H2O2 Faraday efficiency (FE) of CA-OCDs can be up to 97 %, which also has good stability. It is found that the reactivity and selectivity of the CDs are positively correlated with the oxygen content, and the excellent 2e ORR performance is attributed to the COOH groups. The carboxyl group can significantly regulate the oxygen reduction activity by adjusting the electronic structure of the CDs and the charge of the active sites, thus regulating the *OOH adsorption energy. This work develops a facile method to modulate surface of CDs with high H2O2 selectivity and production, and provides new insights into the catalytic mechanisms of carbon-based materials for H2O2 electrical production.
